papergraph.title: Atomic Resolution Structural and Chemical Imaging Revealing the Sequential Migration of Ni, Co, and Mn upon the Battery Cycling of Layered Cathode
papergraph.citation: P. Yan et al. (2017) Atomic Resolution Structural and Chemical Imaging Revealing the Sequential Migration of Ni, Co, and Mn upon the Battery Cycling of Layered Cathode. doi:10.1021/acs.nanolett.7b01546
papergraph.abstract: Layered lithium transition metal oxides (LTMO) are promising candidate cathode materials for next-generation high-energy density lithium ion battery. The challenge for using this category of cathode is the capacity and voltage fading, which is believed to be associated with the layered structure disordering, a process that is initiated from the surface or solid-electrolyte interface and facilitated by transition metal (TM) reduction and oxygen vacancy formation. However, the atomic level dynamic mechanism of such a layered structure disordering is still not fully clear. In this work, utilizing atomic resolution electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS), we map, for the first time at atomic scale, the spatial evolution of Ni, Co and Mn in a cycled LiNi1/3Mn1/3Co1/3O2 layered cathode. In combination with atomic level structural imaging, we discovered the direct correlation of TM ions migration behavior with lattice disordering, featuring the residing of TM ions in the tetrahedral site and a sequential migration of Ni, Co, and Mn upon the increased lattice disordering of the layered structure. This work highlights that Ni ions, though acting as the dominant redox species in many LTMO, are labile to migrate to cause lattice disordering upon battery cycling, while the Mn ions are more stable as compared with Ni and Co and can act as pillar to stabilize layered structure. Direct visualization of the behavior of TM ions during the battery cycling provides insight for designing of cathode with high structural stability and correspondingly a superior performance.

papergraph.title: Modulating Surface Anionic Redox Chemistry toward Highly Stable Li-Rich Cathodes with Negligible Oxygen Loss.
papergraph.citation: (2025) Modulating Surface Anionic Redox Chemistry toward Highly Stable Li-Rich Cathodes with Negligible Oxygen Loss. doi:10.1021/acsnano.5c00630
papergraph.abstract: Low initial Coulombic efficiency and severe capacity/voltage fading during cycling caused by serious irreversible oxygen release, especially in the initial cycle, and resultantly induced unstable electrode/electrolyte interfacial chemistry, largely prohibit the commercial application of high-capacity Li-rich layered oxide cathodes (LLOs). In this work, a dual reductive gas interface cotreatment strategy is applied to regulate the lattice oxygen redox activity and reversibility with a multiple defective structure design including Li/O/TM (TM = transition metal) vacancies and the intrinsic TM doping as well as a full-surface protective layer, which can suppress the irreversible TM migration and then undesirable phase transformation, resisting the corrosion of electrolyte during cycling effectively. Importantly, the introduced reversible SO32-/SO42- redox couple that provides extra capacity compensation could alleviate the distortion of oxygen-central octahedral structure and structural collapse caused by immoderate oxygen oxidation. Thus, the lattice oxygen redox chemistry is optimized, with negligible oxygen loss during the initial cycle. And the designed AS-LLO cathode with greatly enhanced structure stability shows high-capacity retentions of 99.2% at 0.3C after 100 cycles and 82.4% even after 1000 cycles at 5C. This work provides a guideline for manipulating the oxygen redox chemistry to achieve long-lifespan Li-rich layered oxide cathodes for high-energy-density lithium ba...[truncated]

papergraph.title: Aluminum oxide coatings on Co-rich cathodes and interactions with organic electrolyte
papergraph.citation: M. D. Hashan C. Peiris et al. (2025) Aluminum oxide coatings on Co-rich cathodes and interactions with organic electrolyte arXiv:2506.09252v1
papergraph.abstract: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have become essential in modern energy storage; however, their performance is often limited by the stability and efficiency of their components, particularly the cathode and electrolyte. Transition metal layered oxide cathodes, a popular choice for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), suffer from several degradation mechanisms, including capacity fading, reactions with the electrolyte, unstable cathode-electrolyte interfaces, and lattice breakdown during cycling. In recent years, oxide coating, such as alumina, has emerged as a promising strategy to enhance the durability of cathodes by forming a protective layer that mitigates detrimental reactions and improves the stability of the cathode electrolyte interphase (CEI). This study employs ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) simulations to investigate the chemical and mechanical behavior of LiCoO2 cathodes with and without aluminum oxide coatings in contact with an organic electrolyte. We examine the interactions between electrolyte molecules with both bare and coated cathode surfaces, focusing on the decomposition of ethylene carbonate (EC) and dimethyl carbonate (DMC), the formation of oxygen species, and solvation dynamics, and evaluate the mechanical robustness of the cathode-coating interface using calculations of axial strain and cleavage energy. Our findings reveal that alumina coatings effectively reduce electrolyte degradation and stabilize the cathode structure, particularly under high-charge states. The coating's thickness and structural orientation are crucial in enhancing mechanical strength and minimizing detrimental reactions at the cathode-electrolyte interface. These insights contribute to the development of more durable LIBs by optimizing the interface chemistry and mechanical properties, providing a pathway toward higher energy densities and longer cycle life.

papergraph.title: Theory of layered-oxide cathode degradation in Li-ion batteries by oxidation-induced cation disorder
papergraph.citation: Debbie Zhuang et al. (2022) Theory of layered-oxide cathode degradation in Li-ion batteries by oxidation-induced cation disorder doi:10.1149/1945-7111/ac9a09
papergraph.abstract: Disorder-driven degradation phenomena, such as structural phase transformations and surface reconstructions, can significantly reduce the lifetime of Li-ion batteries, especially those with nickel-rich layered-oxide cathodes. We develop a general free energy model for layered-oxide ion-intercalation materials as a function of the degree of disorder, which represents the density of defects in the host crystal. The model accounts for defect core energies, long-range dipolar electrostatic forces, and configurational entropy of the solid solution. In the case of nickel-rich oxides, we hypothesize that nickel with a high concentration of defects is driven into the bulk by electrostatic forces as oxidation reactions at the solid-electrolyte interface reduce nickel and either evolve oxygen gas or oxidize the organic electrolyte at high potentials (>4.4V vs. Li/Li+). The model is used in battery cycling simulations to describe the extent of cathode degradation when using different voltage cutoffs, in agreement with experimental observations that lower-voltage cycling can substantially reduce cathode degradation. The theory provides a framework to guide the development of cathode compositions, coatings and electrolytes to enhance rate capability and enhance battery lifetime. The general theory of cation-disorder formation may also find applications in electrochemical water treatment and ion separations, such as lithium extraction from brines, based on competitive ion intercalation in battery materials.

papergraph.title: Observing Nucleation and Crystallization of Rocksalt LiF from Molten State through Molecular Dynamics Simulations with Refined Machine-Learned Force Field
papergraph.citation: Boyuan Xu et al. (2025) Observing Nucleation and Crystallization of Rocksalt LiF from Molten State through Molecular Dynamics Simulations with Refined Machine-Learned Force Field arXiv:2504.12668v2
papergraph.abstract: Lithium fluoride (LiF) is a critical component for stabilizing lithium metal anode and high-voltage cathodes towards the next-generation high-energy-density lithium batteries. Recent modeling study reported the formation of wurtzite LiF below about 550 K (J. Am. Chem. Soc. 2023, 145, 1327-1333), in contrast to experimental observation of rocksalt LiF under ambient conditions. To address this discrepancy, we employ molecular dynamics (MD) simulations with a refined machine-learned force field (MLFF), and demonstrate the nucleation and crystallization of rocksalt LiF from the molten phase at temperatures below about 800 K. The rocksalt phase remains stable in LiF nanoparticles. Complementary density functional theory (DFT) calculations show that dispersion interactions are essential for correctly predicting the thermodynamic stability of rocksalt LiF over the wurtzite phase on top of the commonly used PBE functional. Furthermore, we show that inclusion of virial stresses--alongside energies and forces--in the training of MLFFs is crucial for capturing phase nucleation and crystallization of rocksalt LiF under the isothermal-isobaric ensemble. These findings underscore the critical role of dispersion interactions in atomistic simulations of battery materials, where such effects are often non-negligible, and highlight the necessity of incorporating virial stresses during the training of MLFF to enable accurate modeling of solid-state systems.
strengths: demonstration of rocksalt LiF nucleation and crystallization from molten phase via MD with refined MLFF
identification of dispersion interactions as essential for thermodynamic stability prediction
highlighting importance of virial stress inclusion in MLFF training
improvements: incorporate virial stresses alongside energies and forces in MLFF training
include dispersion corrections in DFT functionals for accurate phase stability prediction

papergraph.title: Investigating Cathode Electrolyte Interphase Formation in NMC 811 Primary Particles Through Advanced 4D-STEM ACOM Analysis
papergraph.citation: Kevyn Gallegos-Moncayo et al. (2023) Investigating Cathode Electrolyte Interphase Formation in NMC 811 Primary Particles Through Advanced 4D-STEM ACOM Analysis arXiv:2312.13174v1
papergraph.abstract: The study focuses on NMC811, a promising material for high-capacity batteries, and investigates the challenges associated with its use, specifically the formation of the Cathode Electrolyte Interphase (CEI) layer due to chemical reactions. This layer is a consequence of the position of the LUMO energy level of NMC811 that is close to the HOMO level of liquid electrolyte, resulting in electrolyte oxidation and cathode surface alterations during charging. A stable CEI layer can mitigate further degradation by reducing the interaction between the reactive cathode material and the electrolyte. Our research analyzed the CEI layer on NMC811 using advanced techniques such as 4D-STEM ACOM and STEM- EDX, focusing on the effects of different charging voltages (4.3 V and 4.5 V). The findings revealed varying degrees of degradation and the formation of a fluorine-rich layer on the secondary particles. Detailed analysis showed the composition of this layer differed based on the voltage: only LiF at 4.5 V and a combination of LiF and LiOH at 4.3 V. Despite LiF's known stability as a CEI protective layer, our observations indicate it does not effectively prevent degradation in NMC811. The study concludes that impurities and unwanted chemical reactions leading to suboptimal CEI formation are inevitable. Therefore, future efforts should focus on developing protective strategies for NMC811, such as the use of specific additives or coatings.

papergraph.title: Impact of Innovation and Project Control on Sustainable Project Success: Examining the Mediating Role of Project Governance in Pakistan’s Software Development Sector
papergraph.citation: Adil Ali et al. (2026) Impact of Innovation and Project Control on Sustainable Project Success: Examining the Mediating Role of Project Governance in Pakistan’s Software Development Sector doi:10.63544/ijss.v5i2.270
papergraph.abstract:
In modern project setting especially in the software development industry, organizations are progressively relying on innovation as well as on formal Project Control processes as a means of attaining sustainable project success. Whereas innovation facilitates flexibility, technological progress, and innovative problem solving, Project Control mechanisms uphold discipline, accountability and focus on project goals. Nevertheless, it is essential to strike the balance between these two dimensions, which is a serious issue of project managers. Project governance, in this situation, is turning out to be a key factor in the combination of innovative practices with the formal System of the Project Control to provide viability of the project in the long run. This research proposes the effects of innovation and Project Control on sustainable project success as a mediating variable through project governance. The study concentrates on software development firms that are based in Pakistan. The quantitative research design was adopted, and primary data were gathered using a structured questionnaire on the basis of a five-point Likert scale. Project managers, team members and stakeholders gave 300 valid responses. 
The data were analysed through Structural Equation Modelling (SEM) in STATA 18. The results demonstrate that the positive influence of innovation and Project Control is significant in terms of sustainable project success. Moreover, innovation, control, and sustainable project success are closely connected and mediated by project governance. The findings indicate that the likelihood of successful outcomes of projects- sustainability is greater in those organizations that successfully align innovation abilities and established governance and Project Control systems. 
The research will add value to the existing literature on project management due to the empirical evidence presented in the work within the context of the developing economy and practical guidance to the project management community aiming to optimize the performance of long-term projects in terms of performance through balanced management practices.

papergraph.title: Localized High-Concentration Electrolytes Get More Localized Through Micelle-Like Structures
papergraph.citation: Corey M. Efaw et al. (2023) Localized High-Concentration Electrolytes Get More Localized Through Micelle-Like Structures arXiv:2308.06910v1
papergraph.abstract: Liquid electrolytes in batteries are typically treated as macroscopically homogeneous ionic transport media despite having complex chemical composition and atomistic solvation structures, leaving a knowledge gap of microstructural characteristics. Here, we reveal a unique micelle-like structure in a localized high-concentration electrolyte (LHCE), in which the solvent acts as a surfactant between an insoluble salt in diluent. The miscibility of the solvent with the diluent and simultaneous solubility of the salt results in a micelle-like structure with a smeared interface and an increased salt concentration at the centre of the salt-solvent clusters that extends the salt solubility. These intermingling miscibility effects have temperature dependencies, wherein an exemplified LHCE peaks in localized cluster salt concentration near room temperature and is utilized to form a stable solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) on Li-metal anode. These findings serve as a guide to predicting a stable ternary phase diagram and connecting the electrolyte microstructure with electrolyte formulation and formation protocols to form stable SEI for enhanced battery cyclability.

papergraph.title: Quantifying Degradation Parameters of Single-Crystalline Ni-Rich Cathodes in Lithium-Ion Batteries
papergraph.citation: Wengao Zhao et al. (2023) Quantifying Degradation Parameters of Single-Crystalline Ni-Rich Cathodes in Lithium-Ion Batteries. doi:10.1002/anie.202305281
papergraph.abstract: Single-crystal LiNi1-x-yCoxMnyO2 (SC-NCM, x + y + z = 1) cathodes are renowned for their high structural stability and reduced accumulation of adverse side products during long-term cycling. While advances have been made using SC-NCM cathode materials, careful studies of cathode degradation mechanisms are scarce. Herein, we employed quasi single-crystalline LiNi0.65Co0.15Mn0.20O2 (SC-NCM65) to test the relationship between cycling performance and material degradation for different charge cutoff potentials. The Li/SC-NCM65 cells showed >77% capacity retention below 4.6 V vs. Li+/Li after 400 cycles and revealed a significant decay to 56% for 4.7 V cutoff. We demonstrate that the SC-NCM65 degradation is due to accumulation of rock-salt (NiO) species at the particle surface rather than intragranular cracking or side reactions with the electrolyte. The NiO-type layer formation is also responsible for the strongly increased impedance and transition-metal dissolution. Notably, the capacity loss is found to have a linear relationship with the thickness of the rock-salt surface layer. Density functional theory and COMSOL Multiphysics modelling analysis further indicate that the charge-transfer kinetics is decisive, as the lower lithium diffusivity of the NiO phase hinders charge transport from the surface to the bulk.
improvements: No explicit limitation extracted.

papergraph.title: Decoupling of O3‐Layered Cathode Stability from System‐Driven Thermal Runaway in Sodium‐Ion Batteries
papergraph.citation: Yan Li et al. (2025) Decoupling of O3‐Layered Cathode Stability from System‐Driven Thermal Runaway in Sodium‐Ion Batteries doi:10.1002/celc.202500336
papergraph.abstract: Although next‐generation sodium‐ion batteries (SIBs) possess more stable cathode materials than lithium‐ion batteries (LIBs), thermal runaway (TR) remains a critical barrier to SIB applications. To resolve this safety paradox, atomic‐scale investigations are conducted on the O3‐NaNi1/3Fe1/3Mn1/3O2 (NFM) cathode. Combining accelerating rate calorimetry (ARC) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM), the material‐intrinsic resilience is decoupled from cell‐level failure mechanisms. The ARC analysis revealed high safety metrics of the NFM/hard carbon pouch cells; specifically, the maximum TR temperature (T3) stabilizes at ≈310 °C (vs. >800 °C in Ni‐rich LIBs) and the TR onset time extends to ≈40 h. As demonstrated in the TEM analysis, the NFM cathode maintains its structural integrity at 310 °C under inert conditions, although post‐TR cathodes undergo catastrophic “brush‐like” fragmentation with rock‐salt/spinel phase transformation. This degradation is mechanistically attributed to reductive attack by electrolyte decomposition products and anode‐derived gases (H2/CO), which overwhelm the inherent stability of the cathode. To guarantee the inherent safety of SIBs, SIB design based on cathode thermochemistry alone must shift to the co‐optimization of flame‐retardant electrolytes, gas scavengers, and anode passivation.

papergraph.title: Monitoring the Species Formed at High Cathode Potentials at Ni-Rich Cathodes in Lithium-Ion Batteries Using a Novel Dual Working Electrode
papergraph.citation: Leonhard Johannes Reinschluessel et al. (2024) Monitoring the Species Formed at High Cathode Potentials at Ni-Rich Cathodes in Lithium-Ion Batteries Using a Novel Dual Working Electrode doi:10.1149/ma2024-024408mtgabs
papergraph.abstract:
The pursuit to optimize cathode active materials (CAMs) stands as a paramount endeavor within academia and industry to push for higher energy densities in lithium-ion batteries (LiBs). Predominately, aims have focused on the increase of the Nickel content of CAMs to achieve Ni-rich (> 80%) layered transition metal oxides such as NCAs (LiNixCoyAlzO2, where x+y+z=1), owing to their higher achievable capacity at given cut-off voltages. With the increase in Ni-content, however, the structural stability of these CAMs is significantly decreased at high upper cut-off potentials, resulting in capacity fading.[1] The reduced structural stability is accompanied by the release of reactive oxygen species, occurring at ~80% delithiation.[2,3] The released oxygen can trigger a cascade of follow-up reactions, such as the chemical oxidation of electrolyte solvents, conduction salt decomposition, and transition metal (TM) dissolution.[4,5] Charging to even higher voltages (> 4.7 V vs. Li+/Li at 25°C) induces the so called electrochemical oxidation of the electrolyte, which as a result of its reactive reaction products, can induce further CAM degradation.[6-8]

 These reactions significantly impede the lifetime of batteries, and hence require in depth understanding of their underlying mechanisms to establish improved cell chemistries which enable high voltage operation of cells. On-line electrochemical mass spectrometry (OEMS) for the detection of gas evolution, or synchrotron operando X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) for the detection of TM-dissolution delivered valuable insights in the mechanistic understanding.[9] However, as these methods are very time and cost-intensive, a simplified cell design based on a dual working electrode setup was developed by our group, which enables the investigation of parasitic reactions without the use of complex auxiliary devices. In its first proof-of-concept application, it enabled the quantification of oxygen release via the measurement of reductive currents at an sense carbon electrode.[9]

 In the present study, we apply a three-electrode setup for the qualitative analysis of parasitic species formed when Ni-rich cathodes are charged to high cell voltages in LiBs. As in our previous study, the working electrode (WE) is based on LiNi0.80Co0.15Al0.05O2 (NCA; BASF TODA Battery Materials LLC, Japan) and the sense electrode (SE) is a carbon black based electrode (Vulcan-type carbon, XC-72, Tanaka, Japan), both coated on a stainless-steel mesh. As counter electrode (CE), a lithium iron phosphate (LFP) based electrode, delithiated to a degree of 90% and capacitively oversized was used, providing a constant CE potential (at ~3.43 V vs. Li+/Li). This setup allows us to cycle the NCA WE within a desired potential window, while holding the carbon SE at a constant voltage against the LFP CE. Hence, the SE can be used to reduce the species formed at the NCA WE, with the reduction current being a measure of their quantity. The experiments were conducted in a LP47 electrolyte (1 M LiPF6 in EC:DEC (3:7)).
 We apply the developed cell setup to study the formation of O2, H2O, H+, HF, and dissolved transition metal species (TMz+) while the NCA WE is charged to 5.0 V vs. Li+/Li via an intermittent CV charging protocol at temperatures of 10, 25, and 45°C by holding the sense electrode at potentials where the parasitic reactions of interest can be probed. The hereby derived reductive currents are compared to temperature dependent gassing traces determined via OEMS, as well as the TM dissolution behavior measured via operando XAS.

papergraph.title: NMC Family Materials Degradation and Refurbishing
papergraph.citation: Magdalena Winkowska-Struzik et al. (2025) NMC Family Materials Degradation and Refurbishing. doi:10.1149/ma2025-015615mtgabs
papergraph.abstract: In recent years, a type of layered oxide, LiNixMnyCozO2, abbreviated as NMC (where x+y+z=1), has become the preferred cathode material for electric vehicle (EV) batteries. Despite some disorder in the crystal structure due to Li+/Ni2+ cation mixing, the composition offers a high specific capacity of up to 200 mAh g-1 at 4.3 V vs. Li/Li+, 1 however, Ni-rich NMCs have disadvantages, such as lattice oxygen release and material degradation during deep discharge. Moreover, the material can react with ambient air to form surface impurities, complicating slurry preparation and further battery performance. 2–4 The study aims to comprehensively evaluate the structural, morphological, and electrochemical changes in the NMC family (111, 622, and 811) after storage under ambient conditions for at least one month. 5 Our findings are of immense significance as they focus on the possible challenges that may arise due to ambient-air lithium extraction from NMC materials. However, there is no consensus in the literature on whether lithium on the NMC surface can harm the material's structural and electrochemical properties, reduce performance and safety concerns, or benefit its protective properties, especially for Ni-rich NMC. 6–8 An in-depth analysis of the electrode materials can help to understand why certain NMC compositions are more prone to structural degradation upon ambient air storage. Along with our aging studies, we used a degraded commercially available NMC622 as model material and reinserted lithium ions into the NMC structure to obtain refurbished NMC622. We have applied high-temperature treatment on degraded NMC622 to reintroduce lithium from the lithium residual compound. For the comparison, we used an external source of lithium ions, like LiOH or Li2CO3, to provide the correct molar ratio of Li:transition metals. With this presentation, we will show the NMC degradation mechanism of typically used NMC materials, another approach to direct recycling protocol for exemplary NMC622 material, the possibilities of restoring its structure, and the effects of those procedures on the electrochemical performance of the refurbished materials.

papergraph.title: Electrochemical Removal of HF from Carbonate-based $LiPF_6$-containing Li-ion Battery Electrolytes
papergraph.citation: Xiaokun Ge et al. (2024) Electrochemical Removal of HF from Carbonate-based $LiPF_6$-containing Li-ion Battery Electrolytes arXiv:2401.02267v1
papergraph.abstract: Due to the hydrolytic instability of $LiPF_6$ in carbonate-based solvents, HF is a typical impurity in Li-ion battery electrolytes. HF significantly influences the performance of Li-ion batteries, for example by impacting the formation of the solid electrolyte interphase at the anode and by affecting transition metal dissolution at the cathode. Additionally, HF complicates studying fundamental interfacial electrochemistry of Li-ion battery electrolytes, such as direct anion reduction, because it is electrocatalytically relatively unstable, resulting in LiF passivation layers. Methods to selectively remove ppm levels of HF from $LiPF_6$-containing carbonate-based electrolytes are limited. We introduce and benchmark a simple yet efficient electrochemical in situ method to selectively remove ppm amounts of HF from $LiPF_6$-containing carbonate-based electrolytes. The basic idea is the application of a suitable potential to a high surface-area metallic electrode upon which only HF reacts (electrocatalytically) while all other electrolyte components are unaffected under the respective conditions.
improvements: No explicit limitation extracted.
papergraph.contribution: introduction and benchmarking of a simple yet efficient electrochemical in situ method to selectively remove ppm amounts of HF from $LiPF_6$-containing carbonate-based electrolytes

papergraph.title: Voltage Mining for (De)lithiation-stabilized Cathodes and a Machine Learning Model for Li-ion Cathode Voltage
papergraph.citation: Haoming Howard Li et al. (2024) Voltage Mining for (De)lithiation-stabilized Cathodes and a Machine Learning Model for Li-ion Cathode Voltage arXiv:2409.06921v1
papergraph.abstract: Advances in lithium-metal anodes have inspired interest in discovery of Li-free cathodes, most of which are natively found in their charged state. This is in contrast to today's commercial lithium-ion battery cathodes, which are more stable in their discharged state. In this study, we combine calculated cathode voltage information from both categories of cathode materials, covering 5577 and 2423 total unique structure pairs, respectively. The resulting voltage distributions with respect to the redox pairs and anion types for both classes of compounds emphasize design principles for high-voltage cathodes, which favor later Period 4 transition metals in their higher oxidation states and more electronegative anions like fluorine or polyaion groups. Generally, cathodes that are found in their charged, delithiated state are shown to exhibit voltages lower than those that are most stable in their lithiated state, in agreement with thermodynamic expectations. Deviations from this trend are found to originate from different anion distributions between redox pairs. In addition, a machine learning model for voltage prediction based on chemical formulae is constructed, and shows state-of-the-art performance when compared to two established composition-based ML models for materials properties predictions, Roost and CrabNet.
improvements: No explicit limitation extracted.
papergraph.method: machine learning model for voltage prediction based on chemical formulae
papergraph.contribution: A machine learning model for Li-ion cathode voltage prediction showing state-of-the-art performance compared to Roost and CrabNet

papergraph.title: Mitigating Capacity Degradation and Surface Oxygen Loss of Single-Crystalline LiNiO2 Cathodes Via Al2O3 Surface Coating
papergraph.citation: Muhammad Ans et al. (2024) Mitigating Capacity Degradation and Surface Oxygen Loss of Single-Crystalline LiNiO2 Cathodes Via Al2O3 Surface Coating doi:10.1149/ma2024-025576mtgabs
papergraph.abstract:
The performance of next-generation Li-ion batteries (LIBs) for electric vehicle applications relies on energy-dense and low-cost lithium-ion chemistries. Single-crystalline LiNiO2 (SC-LNO) cathodes are regarded as promising materials for high-energy-density applications owing to their high practical capacity, resistance to particle cracking and competitive cost [1]. However, under high voltage operation (≥ 4.4 vs. graphite), they experience rapid capacity loss, owing to parasitic electrode-electrolyte reactions expedited by surface oxygen loss [2]. Overcoming this, stabilising the long-duration performance of SC-LNO cathodes to operate at high voltages while maintaining capacity retention is critical for their integration into commercial batteries. Surface engineering by coating could provide an effective approach to tune the electrochemistry of Ni-rich cathodes while inhibiting degradation pathways [3,4]. Aluminium oxide (Al2O3) coating is used as a barrier to eliminate highly reactive electrolyte species (HF) from the cathode surface, thereby enhancing long-term stability and improving high voltage performance [5,6]. It offers a viable strategy to control the surface of SC-LNO from O loss (O-n, n > 2) and protect it from electrolyte attack. In this study, we employ an ultrathin and conformal film of AlOx on SC-LNO powder via Powder Atomic Layer Deposition (P-ALD). To minimise moisture exposure, the slurry and casting processes were performed in a controlled dry room environment. Electrochemical testing was carried out in balanced coin cells (vs. commercial graphite) at 25 oC and cycled between a voltage range of 2.5–4.2 V and 2.5–4.4 V. Following two formation cycles performed at a C/20 rate, the cells were cycled 100 times at C/3 rate. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy measurements were also performed before and after 100 cycles to assess any impedance changes.
 The electrochemical data revealed that AlOx coated SC-LNO cathodes provide significantly higher capacity retention (94.5%) than uncoated ones (66.3%) after 50 cycles at 4.4 V (vs. graphite). Regarding the rate capability test, the coated cathode delivers improved and stable capacities at faster rates compared with uncoated SC-LNO cathodes. Online electrochemical mass spectroscopy (OEMS) analysis of the AlOx coated cathodes indicates that the onset potential for oxygen loss related gas evolution is > 4.4 V vs. Li/Li+. In contrast, for the uncoated SC-LNO sample, this was found to be ~4.2 V. Hence, the AlOx coating serves as a protective layer, for the cathode against electrolyte-induced degradation and transition metal dissolution at high voltage by forming Al-O-F species at the surface [6]. This beneficial interaction enhances the electrochemical performance by effectively inhibiting impedance growth and improving the cycling life and stability of SC-LNO cathodes. This study highlights the crucial role of AlOx coatings in mitigating surface oxygen loss, which is a key factor contributing to the deterioration of cell performance and issues related to Li+ kinetics. Our findings significantly contribute to advancing the development of high-performance SC-LNO cathode materials, offering valuable insights into the application of ALD coatings for enhancing the performance of Li-ion battery cells.

delayed onset of oxygen-loss-related gas evolution (>4.4 V vs. ~4.2 V)
improvements: minimise moisture exposure during slurry and casting
use controlled dry room environment

papergraph.title: Modern Solid Electrolytes for All-Solid-State Batteries: Materials Chemistry, Structure, and Transport
papergraph.citation: Denys Butenko et al. (2026) Modern Solid Electrolytes for All-Solid-State Batteries: Materials Chemistry, Structure, and Transport arXiv:2604.17380v1
papergraph.abstract: In this review, from crystallographic symmetry to amorphous local polyhedra arrangement and combinations, we examine inorganic solid state electrolytes through the lens of structure property relationships, with oxides, sulfides, and halides representing three major framework chemistries. Halide solid electrolytes and their derivatives, including mixed anion halides and antiperovskite related materials, have expanded this landscape further by introducing new ways to regulate local coordination chemistry, defect populations, and transport active frameworks. Across these families, fast ion conduction depends not simply on composition or crystallographic diffusion pathways, but on the coupled effects of framework topology, site energy distribution, defect chemistry, bottleneck response, and local anion flexibility. Oxides illustrate transport within chemically robust but geometrically constrained frameworks. Sulfides demonstrate that a soft, easily polarizable lattice can broaden the array of low energy migration pathways. Halides occupy an intermediate state, in which the closely packed anion sublattices, an approximately degenerate lithium environment, and mixed anion coordination enable effective transport while simultaneously enhancing oxidation stability and compatibility with cathodes. Building on these comparisons, we argue that long range ion transport is increasingly understood not as motion along a single idealized pathway, but as the macroscopic outcome of statistically connected low barrier local migration events distributed across the structure. We further discuss the experimental and computational approaches required to establish such multiscale structure property relationships and outline future strategies for designing transport active frameworks in which conductivity, stability, and processability are optimized together.
improvements: No explicit limitation extracted.

evidence.title: Quantifying Degradation Parameters of Single-Crystalline Ni-Rich Cathodes in Lithium-Ion Batteries
evidence.citation: Wengao Zhao et al. (2023) Quantifying Degradation Parameters of Single-Crystalline Ni-Rich Cathodes in Lithium-Ion Batteries. doi:10.1002/anie.202305281
evidence.contribution: Demonstrates that degradation of SC-NCM65 is driven by rock-salt (NiO) surface layer accumulation—not intragranular cracking or electrolyte side reactions—and reveals a linear relationship between capacity loss and rock-salt layer thickness.
evidence.limitation: Density functional theory and COMSOL Multiphysics modelling analysis further indicate that the charge-transfer kinetics is decisive, as the lower lithium diffusivity of the NiO phase hinders charge transport from the surface to the bulk.

keynotes.title: Atomic Resolution Structural and Chemical Imaging Revealing the Sequential Migration of Ni, Co, and Mn upon the Battery Cycling of Layered Cathode

keynote.title: Atomic Resolution Structural and Chemical Imaging Revealing the Sequential Migration of Ni, Co, and Mn upon the Battery Cycling of Layered Cathode
keynote.core_problem: Unclear atomic-level dynamic mechanism of layered structure disordering in LiNi1/3Mn1/3Co1/3O2 cathodes during battery cycling, particularly regarding transition metal (TM) ion migration and its causal relationship with capacity/voltage fading.
contributions: First atomic-resolution mapping of spatial evolution of Ni, Co, and Mn in cycled LiNi1/3Mn1/3Co1/3O2 using EELS
Discovery of direct correlation between TM ion migration behavior and lattice disordering
Identification of sequential migration order: Ni → Co → Mn with increasing lattice disordering
Observation of TM ions occupying tetrahedral sites during disordering
Demonstration that Ni is labile and drives disordering, while Mn acts as a structural pillar
methods: Atomic resolution electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS)
Atomic-level structural imaging (implied high-resolution TEM/STEM)
experiments_or_evidence: EELS mapping of Ni, Co, Mn distribution in cycled LiNi1/3Mn1/3Co1/3O2 cathode particles
Correlative structural imaging showing TM occupancy in tetrahedral sites
Analysis linking degree of lattice disordering to extent and order of TM migration
assumptions: Layered structure disordering initiates at surface or solid-electrolyte interface
TM reduction and oxygen vacancy formation facilitate disordering
Observed migration behavior in ex situ sampled cycled material reflects in operando processes
limitations: Ex situ characterization may not capture dynamic, transient migration states
Limited to LiNi1/3Mn1/3Co1/3O2 — generalizability to other LTMOs not demonstrated
No quantification of migration kinetics or thermodynamic driving forces provided
No direct evidence linking specific migration events to electrochemical performance metrics (e.g., voltage fade magnitude)

gap_signals.text: Limited to LiNi1/3Mn1/3Co1/3O2 — generalizability to other LTMOs not demonstrated
No direct evidence linking specific migration events to electrochemical performance metrics (e.g., voltage fade magnitude)
No quantification of migration kinetics or thermodynamic driving forces provided
Ex situ characterization may not capture dynamic, transient migration states
Utilizing atomic resolution electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS), we map, for the first time at atomic scale, the spatial evolution of Ni, Co and Mn...
This work highlights that Ni ions... are labile to migrate... while the Mn ions are more stable... and can act as pillar to stabilize layered structure.
The atomic level dynamic mechanism of such a layered structure disordering is still not fully clear.

important_claims.claim: Ni migrates first, followed by Co, then Mn, in sequence with increasing lattice disordering.
important_claims.evidence: Atomic-resolution EELS mapping combined with structural imaging revealed sequential migration correlated with progressive lattice disordering.

important_claims.claim: Ni ions occupy tetrahedral sites during disordering and drive structural degradation.
important_claims.evidence: Direct visualization showed TM ions residing in tetrahedral sites; Ni identified as dominant redox species yet most labile.

important_claims.claim: Mn ions are structurally more stable than Ni and Co and act as pillars stabilizing the layered framework.
important_claims.evidence: Mn migration occurred last and least; comparative stability inferred from migration sequence and site occupancy observations.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides an atomic-scale observational benchmark for validating computational models of TM migration; informs rational design of Mn-rich or Ni-suppressed LTMO cathodes; establishes EELS + structural imaging as a template protocol for studying cation dynamics in battery electrodes.

keynotes.title: Modulating Surface Anionic Redox Chemistry toward Highly Stable Li-Rich Cathodes with Negligible Oxygen Loss.

keynote.title: Modulating Surface Anionic Redox Chemistry toward Highly Stable Li-Rich Cathodes with Negligible Oxygen Loss
keynote.core_problem: Irreversible oxygen release in Li-rich layered oxide cathodes (LLOs) causes low initial Coulombic efficiency, severe capacity/voltage fading, and unstable electrode/electrolyte interfacial chemistry — hindering commercial application.
contributions: Dual reductive gas interface cotreatment strategy to regulate lattice oxygen redox activity and reversibility
Multiple defective structure design: Li/O/TM vacancies + intrinsic TM doping + full-surface protective layer
Introduction of reversible SO32-/SO42- redox couple for capacity compensation and structural stabilization
Achievement of negligible oxygen loss during the initial cycle
Demonstration of high-capacity retention: 99.2% at 0.3C after 100 cycles and 82.4% at 5C after 1000 cycles
methods: Dual reductive gas interface cotreatment
Design of multiple defects: Li vacancies, O vacancies, TM vacancies, and intrinsic TM doping
Construction of full-surface protective layer
Integration of reversible SO32-/SO42- redox couple
experiments_or_evidence: Electrochemical cycling showing 99.2% capacity retention after 100 cycles at 0.3C
Electrochemical cycling showing 82.4% capacity retention after 1000 cycles at 5C
Evidence of negligible oxygen loss during the initial cycle (stated but no measurement method specified)

gap_signals.text: Prior strategies fail to simultaneously suppress irreversible TM migration, phase transformation, electrolyte corrosion, and oxygen loss.

important_claims.claim: The dual reductive gas interface cotreatment with multiple defective structure design suppresses irreversible TM migration and undesirable phase transformation.
important_claims.evidence: Stated as functional outcome enabling resistance to electrolyte corrosion and improved structural stability.

important_claims.claim: The introduced reversible SO32-/SO42- redox couple alleviates distortion of oxygen-central octahedral structure and structural collapse.
important_claims.evidence: Attributed to mitigating immoderate oxygen oxidation; no direct structural characterization evidence cited in excerpt.

important_claims.claim: The AS-LLO cathode exhibits negligible oxygen loss during the initial cycle.
important_claims.evidence: Stated as a result of optimized lattice oxygen redox chemistry; no experimental quantification method or data provided in excerpt.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a materials design framework—combining defect engineering, surface protection, and auxiliary redox couples—for stabilizing anionic redox in Li-rich cathodes; methodology may generalize to other oxygen-redox-active battery materials.

keynotes.title: Aluminum oxide coatings on Co-rich cathodes and interactions with organic electrolyte

keynote.title: Aluminum oxide coatings on Co-rich cathodes and interactions with organic electrolyte
keynote.core_problem: Degradation of Co-rich layered oxide cathodes (e.g., LiCoO2) in lithium-ion batteries due to electrolyte decomposition, unstable cathode-electrolyte interface (CEI), lattice breakdown, and capacity fading—especially under high-charge states.
contributions: Demonstrates via AIMD simulations that Al2O3 coatings reduce electrolyte degradation (EC/DMC decomposition) and stabilize the cathode structure.
Identifies coating thickness and structural orientation as critical factors for mechanical robustness (axial strain, cleavage energy) and interfacial reactivity suppression.
Provides atomistic insight into how Al2O3 modulates solvation dynamics and oxygen species formation at the cathode-electrolyte interface.
methods: Ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) simulations
Analysis of electrolyte molecule (EC, DMC) interactions with bare vs. Al2O3-coated LiCoO2 surfaces
Quantification of axial strain and cleavage energy at the cathode-coating interface
Examination of EC/DMC decomposition pathways, oxygen evolution, and solvation shell dynamics
assumptions: AIMD simulations accurately capture reactive interfacial chemistry and mechanical response under operating conditions.
Model systems (e.g., idealized LiCoO2 surface, Al2O3 coating morphology, electrolyte composition) are representative of real battery interfaces.
Electrochemical effects (e.g., applied potential, Li+ concentration gradients) are approximated or embedded implicitly in simulation setup.
limitations: No experimental validation (e.g., electrochemical testing, XPS, TEM) is reported or described.
Simulations lack explicit modeling of full CEI formation, long-term cycling, or parasitic side reactions beyond initial decomposition events.
Limited scope to LiCoO2 and Al2O3—generalizability to other Co-rich cathodes (e.g., NMC, LCO variants) or alternative coatings is not assessed.

gap_signals.text: Limited scope to LiCoO2 and Al2O3—generalizability to other Co-rich cathodes (e.g., NMC, LCO variants) or alternative coatings is not assessed.
No experimental validation (e.g., electrochemical testing, XPS, TEM) is reported or described.

important_claims.claim: Alumina coatings effectively reduce electrolyte degradation and stabilize the cathode structure, particularly under high-charge states.
important_claims.evidence: AIMD simulations show suppressed EC/DMC decomposition and reduced oxygen species formation on coated vs. bare LiCoO2 surfaces.

important_claims.claim: Coating thickness and structural orientation are crucial for enhancing mechanical strength and minimizing detrimental interfacial reactions.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: High-value computational reference for interface design in coated cathodes; provides testable hypotheses on coating crystallinity, stoichiometry, and strain engineering—but requires experimental follow-up for validation and translation.

keynotes.title: Theory of layered-oxide cathode degradation in Li-ion batteries by oxidation-induced cation disorder

keynote.title: Theory of layered-oxide cathode degradation in Li-ion batteries by oxidation-induced cation disorder
keynote.core_problem: Disorder-driven degradation (e.g., structural phase transformations, surface reconstructions) in nickel-rich layered-oxide cathodes reduces Li-ion battery lifetime, especially at high voltages (>4.4 V vs. Li/Li+).
contributions: A general free energy model for layered-oxide ion-intercalation materials as a function of cation disorder (defect density)
Incorporation of defect core energies, long-range dipolar electrostatic forces, and configurational entropy
Hypothesis linking interfacial oxidation reactions to Ni migration into the bulk via electrostatic driving forces
Framework to guide design of cathode compositions, coatings, and electrolytes for improved lifetime and rate capability
Potential extension of the theory to electrochemical water treatment and ion separations (e.g., Li extraction from brines)
methods: Free energy modeling of layered-oxide materials parameterized by degree of cation disorder
Inclusion of defect core energy, dipolar electrostatic interactions, and solid-solution configurational entropy
Battery cycling simulations using the model to predict degradation under different voltage cutoffs
experiments_or_evidence: Agreement between model predictions and experimental observations that lower-voltage cycling reduces cathode degradation
assumptions: Oxidation reactions at the solid-electrolyte interface reduce nickel and cause either oxygen evolution or organic electrolyte oxidation
Electrostatic forces drive Ni with high defect concentration into the bulk
Cation disorder is the primary driver of structural degradation in nickel-rich layered oxides

gap_signals.text: We develop a general free energy model... as a function of the degree of disorder, which represents the density of defects in the host crystal.
Disorder-driven degradation phenomena... can significantly reduce the lifetime of Li-ion batteries, especially those with nickel-rich layered-oxide cathodes.
The general theory of cation-disorder formation may also find applications in electrochemical water treatment and ion separations, such as lithium extraction from brines...

important_claims.claim: Lower-voltage cycling substantially reduces cathode degradation.
important_claims.evidence: Model predictions in battery cycling simulations are in agreement with experimental observations.

important_claims.claim: Oxidation reactions at the solid-electrolyte interface (>4.4 V vs. Li/Li+) trigger Ni migration into the bulk via electrostatic forces.
important_claims.evidence: Hypothesis stated in the text; no direct experimental validation described in provided excerpt.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a thermodynamically grounded, parameterizable free energy framework for predicting cation disorder evolution in intercalation materials—reusable for mechanistic studies of degradation, materials screening, and cross-domain applications like selective ion capture.

keynotes.title: Observing Nucleation and Crystallization of Rocksalt LiF from Molten State through Molecular Dynamics Simulations with Refined Machine-Learned Force Field

keynote.title: Observing Nucleation and Crystallization of Rocksalt LiF from Molten State through Molecular Dynamics Simulations with Refined Machine-Learned Force Field
keynote.core_problem: Discrepancy between prior computational prediction of wurtzite LiF stability below ~550 K and experimental observation of rocksalt LiF under ambient conditions; inability of standard MLFFs to reproduce correct phase nucleation behavior.
contributions: Demonstration of rocksalt LiF nucleation and crystallization from molten phase below ~800 K using MD with refined MLFF
Identification of dispersion interactions as essential for thermodynamic stability ranking (rocksalt vs. wurtzite) beyond PBE functional
Establishment of virial stress inclusion in MLFF training as crucial for accurate phase nucleation modeling under isothermal-isobaric ensemble
methods: Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations with refined machine-learned force field (MLFF)
Complementary density functional theory (DFT) calculations with dispersion correction
Training of MLFF using energies, forces, and virial stresses
experiments_or_evidence: MD simulations showing rocksalt nucleation from melt below ~800 K
DFT calculations confirming dispersion interactions reverse stability order (rocksalt > wurtzite) when added to PBE
Comparison of MLFFs trained with vs. without virial stresses, showing only the former reproduces correct nucleation behavior
assumptions: Roocksalt LiF stability under ambient conditions is experimentally established ground truth
PBE functional alone is insufficient for LiF phase energetics without dispersion correction
Isothermal-isobaric (NPT) ensemble is appropriate for modeling nucleation from melt
limitations: No explicit quantification of temperature/pressure ranges beyond 'below ~800 K'
No discussion of finite-size effects or simulation timescales relative to nucleation kinetics
No validation against experimental nucleation rates or structural metrics (e.g., XRD, TEM)

gap_signals.text: No discussion of finite-size effects or simulation timescales relative to nucleation kinetics
No explicit quantification of temperature/pressure ranges beyond 'below ~800 K'
No validation against experimental nucleation rates or structural metrics (e.g., XRD, TEM)

important_claims.claim: Dispersion interactions are essential for correctly predicting thermodynamic stability of rocksalt LiF over wurtzite on top of PBE
important_claims.evidence: Complementary DFT calculations show dispersion corrections reverse stability ordering

important_claims.claim: Inclusion of virial stresses in MLFF training is crucial for capturing rocksalt LiF nucleation and crystallization under isothermal-isobaric ensemble
important_claims.evidence: Only MLFFs trained with virial stresses reproduce correct nucleation behavior in MD

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Highlights two generalizable methodological requirements for accurate MLFF-based solid-state phase modeling: (1) inclusion of dispersion physics in reference data generation, and (2) incorporation of virial stresses in training targets for NPT simulations.

keynote.core_problem: Uncontrolled and suboptimal Cathode Electrolyte Interphase (CEI) formation on NMC811 cathodes due to electrolyte oxidation driven by energetic alignment (NMC811 LUMO near electrolyte HOMO), leading to surface degradation during charging.

keynotes.title: Quantifying Degradation Parameters of Single-Crystalline Ni-Rich Cathodes in Lithium-Ion Batteries

keynote.title: Quantifying Degradation Parameters of Single-Crystalline Ni-Rich Cathodes in Lithium-Ion Batteries
keynote.core_problem: Lack of careful, mechanistic studies on degradation of single-crystalline Ni-rich cathodes (SC-NCM), particularly how cycling conditions (e.g., charge cutoff voltage) drive specific degradation pathways.
contributions: Identifies rock-salt (NiO) surface layer formation—not intragranular cracking or electrolyte side reactions—as the primary degradation mechanism in SC-NCM65.
Establishes a linear relationship between capacity loss and rock-salt layer thickness.
Links increased impedance and transition-metal dissolution to NiO surface layer growth.
Demonstrates decisive role of charge-transfer kinetics (not bulk diffusion) via DFT and COMSOL modeling, attributable to low Li diffusivity in NiO.
methods: Electrochemical cycling of Li/SC-NCM65 half-cells at different charge cutoff potentials (4.6 V vs. 4.7 V vs. Li+/Li).
Post-cycling materials characterization (implied but not specified: likely TEM/XRD/XPS for rock-salt layer detection; impedance spectroscopy for resistance increase).
Density functional theory (DFT) calculations.
COMSOL Multiphysics modeling of charge-transfer kinetics and lithium transport.
experiments_or_evidence: >77% capacity retention after 400 cycles at ≤4.6 V vs. Li+/Li; decay to 56% at 4.7 V cutoff.
Correlation of capacity loss with rock-salt surface layer thickness.
Attribution of impedance rise and TM dissolution to NiO layer formation.
DFT and COMSOL results showing hindered charge transport due to low Li diffusivity in NiO phase.
assumptions: Degradation is dominated by surface phenomena (rock-salt formation) rather than bulk structural collapse or electrolyte decomposition under tested conditions.
Quasi single-crystalline SC-NCM65 is representative of broader SC-NCM behavior.
DFT and COMSOL models accurately capture interfacial charge-transfer limitations imposed by the NiO layer.
limitations: Specific characterization techniques (e.g., TEM, XPS, ICP-MS) are not named—methodological transparency limited.
No details on electrolyte composition, temperature, current rate, or electrode loading—limits generalizability.
Study restricted to one composition (SC-NCM65) and two cutoff voltages—lack of broader voltage/composition sweep.
Half-cell configuration (Li metal anode) does not reflect full-cell degradation dynamics or anode-coupled effects.

keynotes.title: Decoupling of O3‐Layered Cathode Stability from System‐Driven Thermal Runaway in Sodium‐Ion Batteries

keynote.title: Decoupling of O3‐Layered Cathode Stability from System‐Driven Thermal Runaway in Sodium‐Ion Batteries
keynote.core_problem: Thermal runaway remains a critical safety barrier for sodium-ion batteries despite their cathodes being intrinsically more stable than lithium-ion battery cathodes — creating a 'safety paradox'.
contributions: Decoupled material-intrinsic cathode resilience from cell-level thermal runaway mechanisms.

Identified that cathode structural integrity up to ≈310 °C is overwhelmed not by intrinsic instability, but by system-level reductive attack from electrolyte decomposition products and anode-derived gases (H2/CO).
Demonstrated that SIB safety design must shift from cathode thermochemistry-only optimization to co-optimization of flame-retardant electrolytes, gas scavengers, and anode passivation.
methods: Accelerating rate calorimetry (ARC) on NFM/hard carbon pouch cells.
Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) under inert heating up to 310 °C and post-thermal-runaway analysis.
experiments_or_evidence: ARC showed maximum TR temperature (T3) ≈310 °C and TR onset time ≈40 h for NFM/hard carbon pouch cells.
TEM confirmed NFM cathode retains structural integrity at 310 °C under inert conditions.
Post-TR TEM revealed 'brush-like' fragmentation and rock-salt/spinel phase transformation.
Degradation was mechanistically linked to reductive attack by electrolyte decomposition products and anode-derived H2/CO.
assumptions: Inert-condition TEM heating reflects baseline cathode thermal stability independent of electrochemical or system-level side reactions.
Reductive species (H2, CO, electrolyte decomposition products) are primary drivers of post-onset cathode degradation.
limitations: Findings specific to O3-type NaNi1/3Fe1/3Mn1/3O2 (NFM) cathode and hard carbon anode — generalizability to other SIB chemistries untested.
No quantitative modeling or kinetic analysis of gas–cathode interaction provided.
No evaluation of proposed mitigation strategies (e.g., flame-retardant electrolytes, gas scavengers) in experimental validation.

gap_signals.text: Findings specific to O3-type NaNi1/3Fe1/3Mn1/3O2 (NFM) cathode and hard carbon anode — generalizability to other SIB chemistries untested.
No evaluation of proposed mitigation strategies (e.g., flame-retardant electrolytes, gas scavengers) in experimental validation.
No quantitative modeling or kinetic analysis of gas–cathode interaction provided.
Cathode thermochemistry alone is insufficient for ensuring SIB safety; system-level factors (electrolyte decomposition, anode-derived gases) dominate post-onset degradation.

important_claims.claim: NFM cathode maintains structural integrity up to ≈310 °C under inert conditions.
important_claims.evidence: TEM analysis under inert heating confirmed retained crystallinity and absence of phase transformation at 310 °C.

important_claims.claim: Post-thermal-runaway cathode degradation is driven by reductive attack from electrolyte decomposition products and anode-derived gases (H2/CO), not intrinsic cathode instability.
important_claims.evidence: Post-TR TEM showed 'brush-like' fragmentation and rock-salt/spinel phase transformation, attributed mechanistically to reductive species overwhelming inherent cathode stability.

important_claims.claim: Thermal runaway onset in SIBs occurs at significantly lower temperature (≈310 °C) and later time (≈40 h) than in Ni-rich LIBs (>800 °C).
important_claims.evidence: ARC analysis of NFM/hard carbon pouch cells yielded T3 ≈310 °C and onset time ≈40 h.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a methodological template (ARC + in situ/inert TEM) for decoupling intrinsic material stability from system-driven failure; highlights critical cross-component coupling (anode→gas→cathode) as a reusable failure mode framework for beyond-lithium battery safety research.

keynotes.title: Monitoring the Species Formed at High Cathode Potentials at Ni-Rich Cathodes in Lithium-Ion Batteries Using a Novel Dual Working Electrode

keynote.title: Monitoring the Species Formed at High Cathode Potentials at Ni-Rich Cathodes in Lithium-Ion Batteries Using a Novel Dual Working Electrode
keynote.core_problem: Understanding and detecting parasitic species (e.g., O2, H2O, HF, TMz+, H+) formed during high-voltage charging (>4.7 V) of Ni-rich cathodes, which drive degradation and limit battery lifetime.
contributions: Development and application of a simplified three-electrode dual-working-electrode cell setup with a carbon sense electrode to qualitatively detect parasitic species via reductive currents
Extension of prior dual-electrode proof-of-concept (which quantified O2 release) to probe multiple species (O2, H2O, H+, HF, TMz+)
Correlation of reductive current signals from the sense electrode with temperature-dependent OEMS gassing data and operando XAS TM dissolution measurements
methods: Three-electrode electrochemical cell with NCA working electrode (LiNi0.80Co0.15Al0.05O2), carbon black (XC-72) sense electrode, and delithiated LFP counter electrode (~3.43 V vs. Li+/Li)
Intermittent CV charging protocol to 5.0 V vs. Li+/Li at 10, 25, and 45°C
Holding sense electrode at specific potentials to selectively reduce target parasitic species and measure corresponding reductive currents
Cross-validation against online electrochemical mass spectrometry (OEMS) and operando X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS)
experiments_or_evidence: Reductive current transients measured at carbon sense electrode during high-voltage NCA charging
Comparison of reductive current profiles with OEMS-derived gassing traces across temperatures
Comparison with TM dissolution kinetics obtained via operando XAS
assumptions: Reductive currents at the carbon sense electrode are attributable solely to electroactive parasitic species generated at the NCA working electrode
Carbon electrode selectivity for reduction of target species (O2, H2O, H+, HF, TMz+) can be tuned by applied potential
LFP counter electrode maintains stable, constant potential during experiments
limitations: Qualitative (not quantitative) analysis of parasitic species — no calibration or absolute concentration determination reported
Carbon electrode lacks chemical specificity; overlapping reduction peaks may hinder unambiguous species assignment
No validation of detection limits or sensitivity for individual species (e.g., HF, H+)
Limited to lab-scale half-cell configuration; relevance to full-cell operation not assessed

gap_signals.text: Limited to lab-scale half-cell configuration; relevance to full-cell operation not assessed
No validation of detection limits or sensitivity for individual species (e.g., HF, H+)
Qualitative (not quantitative) analysis of parasitic species — no calibration or absolute concentration determination reported
Carbon electrode lacks chemical specificity; overlapping reduction peaks may hinder unambiguous species assignment
Prior dual-electrode work only quantified O2; broader detection of co-evolving species (H2O, HF, TMz+, H+) remains unaddressed.

Reduced structural stability of Ni-rich cathodes at high voltage is accompanied by oxygen release at ~80% delithiation, triggering cascading parasitic reactions whose kinetics and interdependencies remain insufficiently resolved.
Existing techniques like OEMS and operando XAS are 'very time and cost-intensive', motivating need for simpler alternatives.

datasets_or_materials: NCA cathode material (LiNi0.80Co0.15Al0.05O2, BASF TODA Battery Materials LLC, Japan)
Carbon black sense electrode (Vulcan XC-72, Tanaka, Japan)
Delithiated LFP counter electrode (90% delithiated, capacitively oversized)
LP47 electrolyte (1 M LiPF6 in EC:DEC, 3:7 vol)

important_claims.claim: The dual-working-electrode setup enables qualitative analysis of multiple parasitic species (O2, H2O, H+, HF, TMz+) via reductive currents at a carbon sense electrode.
important_claims.evidence: Reductive currents were measured during intermittent CV charging to 5.0 V; signals were compared to OEMS gassing and operando XAS TM dissolution.

important_claims.claim: Oxygen release occurs at ~80% delithiation and triggers cascading reactions including electrolyte oxidation and TM dissolution.
important_claims.evidence: Cited references [2,3] for oxygen release timing; [4–8] for downstream reaction pathways.

important_claims.claim: Charging above 4.7 V induces electrochemical oxidation of the electrolyte, generating reactive products that further degrade the cathode.
important_claims.evidence: Cited references [6–8] for electrochemical oxidation onset and consequences.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a low-cost, experimentally accessible electrochemical platform to screen parasitic reaction onset and temperature dependence in Ni-rich cathodes—enabling rapid comparative studies across materials or electrolytes without synchrotron or OEMS infrastructure.

keynotes.title: NMC Family Materials Degradation and Refurbishing

keynote.title: NMC Family Materials Degradation and Refurbishing
keynote.core_problem: Structural, morphological, and electrochemical degradation of NMC cathode materials (111, 622, 811) during ambient-air storage, and uncertainty regarding the role of surface lithium extraction in performance loss, safety, or protection — particularly for Ni-rich compositions.
contributions: Comprehensive evaluation of structural, morphological, and electrochemical changes in NMC-111, NMC-622, and NMC-811 after ≥1 month ambient-air storage
Investigation of ambient-air-induced lithium extraction as a key degradation mechanism
Development and demonstration of a refurbishing protocol for degraded NMC622 via lithium reinsertion using high-temperature treatment with external Li sources (e.g., LiOH, Li2CO3)
Assessment of structural restoration and electrochemical recovery in refurbished NMC622
methods: Ambient-air aging of NMC-111, NMC-622, and NMC-811 for ≥1 month
High-temperature thermal treatment of degraded NMC622 with external lithium sources (LiOH or Li2CO3)
Structural characterization (implied: XRD, SEM/TEM — though not explicitly named in text)
Morphological and electrochemical analysis (implied: capacity, cycling stability — not detailed)
experiments_or_evidence: Comparative aging study across NMC compositions (111, 622, 811)
Refurbishing experiment on commercially degraded NMC622 using LiOH/Li2CO3-assisted high-T treatment
Electrochemical performance testing of refurbished NMC622 (specific metrics not stated)
assumptions: Surface lithium loss during ambient-air exposure is a primary driver of degradation
Lithium reinsertion via external Li source + thermal treatment can restore bulk structure and function
Ni-rich NMCs (e.g., 811) are more susceptible to ambient-air degradation than lower-Ni variants
limitations: No consensus in literature on functional impact of surface Li loss — study does not resolve this ambiguity quantitatively
Specific characterization techniques, quantitative metrics (e.g., % capacity recovery), and experimental conditions (T, time, atmosphere control) are not provided
Refurbishing protocol demonstrated only on NMC622; generalizability to NMC-111/811 or other Ni-rich variants is unstated
No discussion of side reactions, impurity formation during refurbishing, or long-term stability of refurbished material

gap_signals.text: No consensus in literature on functional impact of surface Li loss — study does not resolve this ambiguity quantitatively
No discussion of side reactions, impurity formation during refurbishing, or long-term stability of refurbished material
Refurbishing protocol demonstrated only on NMC622; generalizability to NMC-111/811 or other Ni-rich variants is unstated
Specific characterization techniques, quantitative metrics (e.g., % capacity recovery), and experimental conditions (T, time, atmosphere control) are not provided
Limited exploration of direct recycling/refurbishing as an alternative to conventional hydrometallurgical/pyrometallurgical routes for spent NMC cathodes.

No consensus in the literature on whether lithium on the NMC surface harms structural/electrochemical properties, reduces performance/safety, or confers protective benefits — especially for Ni-rich NMC.
Lack of standardized protocols for evaluating or mitigating ambient-air degradation across NMC compositions.

datasets_or_materials: Degraded commercial NMC622 (used as model material)

important_claims.claim: Ambient-air storage induces lithium extraction from NMC surface, contributing to degradation.
important_claims.evidence: Study focuses on 'possible challenges that may arise due to ambient-air lithium extraction from NMC materials' and links it to structural/electrochemical changes.

important_claims.claim: Refurbishing degraded NMC622 via lithium reinsertion restores structure and electrochemical performance.
important_claims.evidence: Presentation states intent to show 'possibilities of restoring its structure, and the effects of those procedures on the electrochemical performance of the refurbished materials.'

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a comparative aging framework across major NMC stoichiometries and introduces a direct refurbishing approach applicable to recycling research; however, lack of methodological detail and quantitative results limits immediate reproducibility.

keynotes.title: Electrochemical Removal of HF from Carbonate-based $LiPF_6$-containing Li-ion Battery Electrolytes

keynote.title: Electrochemical Removal of HF from Carbonate-based $LiPF_6$-containing Li-ion Battery Electrolytes
keynote.core_problem: Presence of ppm-level HF impurity in $LiPF_6$-based carbonate electrolytes, causing detrimental effects on battery performance (SEI formation, transition metal dissolution) and confounding fundamental interfacial electrochemistry studies (e.g., direct anion reduction) due to premature LiF passivation.
contributions: Introduction and benchmarking of a simple, efficient electrochemical in situ method for selective removal of ppm-level HF from $LiPF_6$-containing carbonate electrolytes.
Demonstration that HF can be selectively reacted at a high surface-area metallic electrode under a suitably controlled potential, without affecting other electrolyte components.
methods: Electrochemical treatment using a high surface-area metallic electrode.

Application of a carefully selected potential window where only HF undergoes electrocatalytic reaction (yielding LiF), while $LiPF_6$, carbonate solvents, and other electrolyte constituents remain electrochemically inert.
assumptions: HF is electrocatalytically less stable than other electrolyte components under the applied potential conditions.
The metallic electrode provides sufficient surface area and suitable catalytic activity for selective HF reduction without side reactions.
Other electrolyte components (e.g., $LiPF_6$, EC, DMC, EMC) are electrochemically stable within the chosen potential window.

gap_signals.text: HF complicates studying fundamental interfacial electrochemistry [...] because it is electrocatalytically relatively unstable, resulting in LiF passivation layers.
Methods to selectively remove ppm levels of HF from $LiPF_6$-containing carbonate-based electrolytes are limited.

important_claims.claim: HF is electrocatalytically relatively unstable and forms LiF passivation layers, thereby interfering with studies of direct anion reduction.
important_claims.evidence: Full text excerpt: 'HF complicates studying fundamental interfacial electrochemistry of Li-ion battery electrolytes, such as direct anion reduction, because it is electrocatalytically relatively unstable, resulting in LiF passivation layers.'

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Enables cleaner electrolyte systems for mechanistic electrochemical studies; provides a practical, in situ purification strategy applicable to lab-scale electrolyte conditioning and potentially cell manufacturing.

keynotes.title: Voltage Mining for (De)lithiation-stabilized Cathodes and a Machine Learning Model for Li-ion Cathode Voltage

keynote.title: Voltage Mining for (De)lithiation-stabilized Cathodes and a Machine Learning Model for Li-ion Cathode Voltage
keynote.core_problem: Designing high-voltage Li-free cathodes (natively charged/delithiated) that remain stable and perform comparably to conventional lithiated-state-stable cathodes, while enabling compatibility with lithium-metal anodes.
contributions: Compilation and comparative analysis of voltage distributions across 5577 lithiated-state-stable and 2423 delithiated-state-stable cathode structure pairs
Identification of design principles for high-voltage cathodes: preference for later Period 4 transition metals in higher oxidation states and more electronegative anions (e.g., F, polyanions)
Construction of a novel machine learning model for cathode voltage prediction from chemical formulae, outperforming Roost and CrabNet on composition-based voltage prediction
methods: Computational voltage calculation (method unspecified — e.g., DFT-based or thermodynamic formalism implied)
Statistical analysis of voltage distributions stratified by redox pairs and anion types
Machine learning modeling using chemical formulae as input (architecture and featurization not specified)
experiments_or_evidence: Voltage distribution comparison between lithiated-state-stable (5577 structures) and delithiated-state-stable (2423 structures) cathodes
Empirical observation that delithiated-state-stable cathodes generally exhibit lower voltages than lithiated-state-stable ones — consistent with thermodynamics
Quantitative performance evaluation of the ML model against Roost and CrabNet (metric unspecified, but described as 'state-of-the-art')
assumptions: Voltage calculations reliably reflect experimental electrochemical behavior
Thermodynamic expectations (e.g., relative stability dictating voltage trends) hold across diverse redox chemistries and anion environments
Chemical formula alone suffices for accurate voltage prediction without structural or symmetry information

keynotes.title: Mitigating Capacity Degradation and Surface Oxygen Loss of Single-Crystalline LiNiO2 Cathodes Via Al2O3 Surface Coating

keynote.title: Mitigating Capacity Degradation and Surface Oxygen Loss of Single-Crystalline LiNiO2 Cathodes Via Al2O3 Surface Coating
keynote.core_problem: Rapid capacity loss in single-crystalline LiNiO2 (SC-LNO) cathodes under high-voltage operation (≥ 4.4 V vs. graphite), driven by surface oxygen loss and parasitic electrode-electrolyte reactions.
contributions: Demonstration that ultrathin, conformal AlOx coating via Powder Atomic Layer Deposition (P-ALD) significantly improves capacity retention of SC-LNO at 4.4 V
Evidence that AlOx coating raises the onset potential of oxygen-loss-related gas evolution from ~4.2 V to >4.4 V vs. Li/Li+
Identification of Al-O-F surface species formation as a mechanism for suppressing transition metal dissolution and electrolyte-induced degradation
Correlation between suppressed impedance growth and enhanced cycling stability in coated cathodes
methods: Powder Atomic Layer Deposition (P-ALD) for ultrathin, conformal AlOx coating on SC-LNO powder
Electrochemical testing in balanced coin cells vs. commercial graphite at 25 °C, cycled between 2.5–4.2 V and 2.5–4.4 V
Formation cycling at C/20 followed by 100 cycles at C/3 rate
Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) before and after 100 cycles
Online Electrochemical Mass Spectroscopy (OEMS) to detect oxygen-loss-related gas evolution
experiments_or_evidence: 94.5% capacity retention after 50 cycles at 4.4 V for AlOx-coated SC-LNO vs. 66.3% for uncoated
Improved and stable rate capability in coated cathodes compared to uncoated
OEMS shows oxygen-loss onset shifted from ~4.2 V (uncoated) to >4.4 V (coated) vs. Li/Li+
EIS data showing inhibited impedance growth in coated cells
assumptions: AlOx coating acts primarily as a chemical barrier against HF and reactive electrolyte species
Formation of Al-O-F species at the interface is beneficial and stabilising
Moisture exposure during slurry/casting must be minimised to preserve coating integrity
limitations: No data provided on long-term cycling beyond 100 cycles or at elevated temperatures
No quantitative analysis of coating thickness or uniformity (e.g., TEM, XPS depth profiling)
No comparison with other coating materials (e.g., LiPO3, AlF3) or alternative ALD precursors
Coin-cell results may not translate directly to practical pouch-cell performance or calendaring effects

AlOx-coated SC-LNO samples prepared via P-ALD

important_claims.claim: AlOx coating raises oxygen-loss onset potential from ~4.2 V to >4.4 V vs. Li/Li+
important_claims.evidence: OEMS analysis showing delayed gas evolution onset in coated vs. uncoated samples

important_claims.claim: AlOx coating improves capacity retention to 94.5% after 50 cycles at 4.4 V
important_claims.evidence: Electrochemical cycling data comparing coated (94.5%) and uncoated (66.3%) SC-LNO

important_claims.claim: AlOx coating inhibits impedance growth and enhances cycling stability
important_claims.evidence: Pre- and post-cycling EIS measurements showing suppressed impedance rise in coated cells
