keynotes.title: Modern Solid Electrolytes for All-Solid-State Batteries: Materials Chemistry, Structure, and Transport

keynote.title: Modern Solid Electrolytes for All-Solid-State Batteries: Materials Chemistry, Structure, and Transport
keynote.core_problem: Understanding and designing inorganic solid electrolytes for all-solid-state batteries by linking atomic- and mesoscale structural features to macroscopic ionic transport properties.
contributions: Systematic comparative analysis of oxide, sulfide, and halide solid electrolytes through structure–property relationships
Conceptual framing of long-range ion transport as the statistical outcome of distributed low-barrier local migration events
Identification of halides as an intermediate class balancing transport, oxidation stability, and cathode compatibility
methods: Comparative crystallographic analysis across material families (oxides, sulfides, halides)
Examination of local coordination chemistry, defect populations, and anion flexibility
Multiscale analysis spanning crystallographic symmetry to amorphous polyhedral arrangements
assumptions: Ionic transport is governed by coupled effects of framework topology, site energy distribution, defect chemistry, bottleneck response, and local anion flexibility
Macroscopic conductivity emerges statistically from localized, low-barrier migration events rather than single idealized diffusion pathways

gap_signals.text: Long-range ion transport is increasingly understood not as motion along a single idealized pathway, but as the macroscopic outcome of statistically connected low barrier local migration events distributed across the structure.
Strategies for co-optimizing conductivity, stability, and processability in transport-active frameworks remain emergent.
Experimental and computational approaches required to establish multiscale structure–property relationships are underdeveloped.

datasets_or_materials: Halide solid electrolytes (including mixed-anion halides and antiperovskite-related materials)

important_claims.claim: Fast ion conduction depends not simply on composition or crystallographic diffusion pathways, but on the coupled effects of framework topology, site energy distribution, defect chemistry, bottleneck response, and local anion flexibility.
important_claims.evidence: Comparative analysis across oxides, sulfides, and halides showing divergent transport mechanisms despite shared Li-ion conduction goal.

important_claims.claim: Halides occupy an intermediate state enabling effective transport while enhancing oxidation stability and cathode compatibility.
important_claims.evidence: Their closely packed anion sublattices, approximately degenerate lithium environment, and mixed anion coordination collectively support this balance.

important_claims.claim: Oxides exhibit transport within chemically robust but geometrically constrained frameworks.
important_claims.evidence: Contrasted with sulfides and halides in structural–transport comparison.

important_claims.claim: Sulfides demonstrate that a soft, easily polarizable lattice can broaden the array of low-energy migration pathways.
important_claims.evidence: Contrasted with oxides and halides in structural–transport comparison.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a cross-cutting conceptual and comparative framework for rational design of solid electrolytes; identifies key structural descriptors (e.g., anion flexibility, site degeneracy, bottleneck response) that can be reused in computational screening, materials synthesis, and multiscale modeling efforts.

method_scenario_benchmark_triples.scenario: inorganic solid-state electrolytes for all-solid-state batteries
references: Denys Butenko et al. (2026) Modern Solid Electrolytes for All-Solid-State Batteries: Materials Chemistry, Structure, and Transport arXiv:2604.17380v1

evidence_type_annotations.claim: structure–property relationship analysis
Ion transport is the macroscopic outcome of statistically connected low-barrier local migration events distributed across the structure, not motion along a single idealized pathway.

Modern Solid Electrolytes for All-Solid-State Batteries: Materials Chemistry, Structure, and Transport In this review, from crystallographic symmetry to amorphous local polyhedra arrangement and combinations, we examine inorganic solid state electrolytes through the lens of structure property relati...[truncated]
Full text excerpt:

In this review, from crystallographic symmetry to amorphous local polyhedra arrangement and combinations, we examine inorganic solid state electrolytes through the lens of structure property relationships, with oxides, sulfides, and halides representing three major framework chemistries.

Halide solid electrolytes and their derivatives, including mixed anion halides and antiperovskite related materials, have expanded this landscape further by introducing new ways to regulate local coordination chemistry, defect populations, and transport active frameworks.

Across these families, fast ion conduction depends not simply on composition or crystallographic diffusion pathways, but on the coupled effects of framework topology, site energy distribution, defect chemistry, bottleneck response, and local anion flexibility.
Oxides illustrate transport within chemically robust but geometrically constrained frameworks.

We further discuss the experimental and computational approaches required to establish such multiscale structure property relationships and outline future strategies for designing transport active frameworks in which conductivity, stability, and processability are optimized together.

Full text excerpt:

In this review, from crystallographic symmetry to amorphous local polyhedra arrangement and combinations, we examine inorganic solid state electrolytes through the lens of structure property relationships, with oxides, sulfides, and halides representing three major framework chemistries.

Halide solid electrolytes and their derivatives, including mixed anion halides and antiperovskite related materials, have expanded this landscape further by introducing new ways to regulate local coordination chemistry, defect populations, and transport active frameworks.

Across these families, fast ion conduction depends not simply on composition or crystallographic diffusion pathways, but on the coupled effects of framework topology, site energy distribution, defect chemistry, bottleneck response, and local anion flexibility.
Oxides illustrate transport within chemically robust but geometrically constrained frameworks.

We further discuss the experimental and computational approaches required to establish such multiscale structure property relationships and outline future strategies for designing transport active frameworks in which conductivity, stability, and processability are optimized together.

In this work, a dual reductive gas interface cotreatment strategy is applied to regulate the lattice oxygen redox activity and reversibility with a multiple defective structure design including Li/O/TM (TM = transition metal) vacancies and the intrinsic TM doping as well as a full-surface protective...[truncated]

evidence_type_annotations.claim: ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) simulations

Alumina coatings reduce electrolyte degradation and stabilize the cathode structure, particularly under high-charge states; coating thickness and structural orientation critically influence mechanical strength and interfacial reactivity.

Aluminum oxide coatings on Co-rich cathodes and interactions with organic electrolyte Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have become essential in modern energy storage; however, their performance is often limited by the stability and efficiency of their components, particularly the cathode and electrolyte...[truncated]

method_scenario_benchmark_triples.scenario: nickel-rich layered-oxide cathodes in Li-ion batteries
references: Debbie Zhuang et al. (2022) Theory of layered-oxide cathode degradation in Li-ion batteries by oxidation-induced cation disorder doi:10.1149/1945-7111/ac9a09

evidence_type_annotations.claim: free energy model

A general free energy model for layered-oxide ion-intercalation materials as a function of disorder, accounting for defect core energies, long-range dipolar electrostatic forces, and configurational entropy, explaining oxidation-induced cation disorder and cathode degradation.

In the case of nickel-rich oxides, we hypothesize that nickel with a high concentration of defects is driven into the bulk by electrostatic forces as oxidation reactions at the solid-electrolyte interface reduce nickel and either evolve oxygen gas or oxidize the organic electrolyte at high potential...[truncated]
